beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam, in for Bear. Ask Claude to create an agent, add a subagent, write agent frontmatter, or design triggering conditions for a Claude Code plugin — the Agent Development skill fires. Agents are not commands. Agents handle complex multi-step tasks autonomously; commands are user-initiated. An agent is a markdown file: YAML frontmatter with name, description, model, color, and optionally tools, plus a body that becomes the system prompt. The description field is the most critical — it defines triggering conditions and must include two to four example blocks. Let me walk you through the file structure, the frontmatter fields, and where this skill gets it right and where it bites.

props.command: Create an agent for my plugin that reviews Python code for security vulnerabilities.
output: YAML frontmatter: name · description · model · color · tools
description = most critical field: triggering conditions + 2-4 examples

beats.act: anatomy — frontmatter fields + system prompt structure
beats.narration_text: An agent file has two parts. Part one: YAML frontmatter. Name: lowercase hyphens only, three to fifty characters, must start and end with alphanumeric. Description: the triggering field — begins with 'Use this agent when' followed by two to four example blocks, each with Context, user request, assistant response, and commentary explaining why this agent fires. This is the most critical field. Model: inherit is recommended unless you need opus for heavy capability or haiku for speed. Color: six options — blue and cyan for analysis and review; green for success-oriented tasks; yellow for caution and validation; red for critical or security; magenta for creative generation. Tools: optional array — if omitted, the agent has all tools. Listing only the minimum needed is the correct pattern. Part two: the markdown body becomes the system prompt. Write in second person. Structure: Core Responsibilities, Analysis Process, Quality Standards, Output Format, and Edge Cases.

props.sparkLine: Frontmatter triggers. Body becomes the system prompt.

beats.narration_text: The description field is where most agents fail. Without example blocks, Claude cannot learn when to fire the agent. The required format: 'Use this agent when' followed by the conditions, then two to four example blocks. Each block contains Context colon a scenario description, user colon the quoted request, assistant colon the quoted response, and a commentary section explaining why this agent is appropriate. Cover different phrasings of the same intent across your examples, and include at least one example showing when not to use the agent. Two creation paths exist. The AI-assisted path: give Claude a structured prompt requesting identifier, whenToUse, and systemPrompt fields as JSON, then convert the JSON to the agent file format. The manual path: nine steps from choosing identifier through validating with scripts/validate-agent.sh and testing triggering with scripts/test-agent-trigger.sh. Either path ends with the same validation check: does the agent trigger on the phrasing it claims to handle, and does the system prompt produce the expected output?

props.sparkLine: Examples teach triggering. No examples, no trigger.

beats.act: teardown — what it gets right / where it bites
beats.narration_text: Here is the teardown. The Agent Development skill gets agents versus commands right: autonomous multi-step work versus user-initiated actions — stated once, clearly. The description field is correctly identified as the most critical field, and the example block format is fully specified with Context, user, assistant, and commentary. System prompt structure is comprehensive with the five-section template. Validation rules are concrete: name three to fifty characters, description ten to five thousand characters with examples required, system prompt twenty to ten thousand characters. Color semantics map to use case domains. Where it bites: there is no agent-versus-command decision tree — the distinction is stated but no decision criteria help you choose. Model inherit is recommended without any heuristics for when to upgrade to opus. Color semantics are advisory only — no tooling enforces them. validate-agent.sh and test-agent-trigger.sh are referenced but not described anywhere in the skill. And there is no multi-agent coordination guidance — how one agent hands off to another is not covered.

props.sparkLine: Description field nailed. Decision tree missing.

beats.narration_text: Verdict. Agent Development fires for creating agents, subagents, and agent frontmatter in Claude Code plugins. Agents: autonomous multi-step; Commands: user-initiated. Two parts: YAML frontmatter (name, description, model, color, optional tools) and a markdown body that becomes the system prompt. Description is most critical — 'Use this agent when' plus two to four example blocks with Context/user/assistant/commentary. Model defaults to inherit; color has semantic guidelines; tools follow least privilege. System prompt: Responsibilities, Process, Standards, Output, Edge Cases. Validate with scripts/validate-agent.sh. Gaps: no agent-vs-command decision tree, no multi-agent coordination, no model-selection heuristics.

artifactLines: Agents = autonomous multi-step work · Commands = user-initiated actions
File: YAML frontmatter (name · description · model · color · tools?) + markdown body
description = most critical: 'Use this agent when' + 2-4 examples (Context/user/assistant/commentary)
model: inherit by default · color: semantic 6 options · tools: least privilege
System prompt body: Responsibilities → Process → Standards → Output → Edge Cases
Gaps: no agent-vs-command criteria · no model heuristics · no multi-agent coordination

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Open a Claude Code session. Paste this: Create an agent for my plugin that reviews Python code for security vulnerabilities. Watch four things: first, does Claude write a description field that begins with 'Use this agent when' and includes at least two example blocks with Context, user, assistant, and commentary sections? Second, does it set model to inherit rather than hardcoding opus or sonnet? Third, does it list only the tools the agent actually needs — Read and Grep for a code reviewer — rather than omitting the tools field and granting all access? And fourth, does the system prompt body use second person — 'You are' not 'I am' — and include a named Analysis Process section with numbered steps? If the description has no example blocks, Claude will not trigger the agent reliably. If tools is omitted, the agent has full access. Those are your gates.

props.command: Create an agent for my plugin that reviews Python code for security vulnerabilities.
props.runningText: Watch: description has examples? model=inherit? tools restricted? system prompt in 2nd person?
output: Expected: model: inherit, tools: ["Read", "Grep"] or similar minimal set
Red flag: no examples in description, or tools field omitted

beats.narration_text: Agent Development. Liam, in for Bear.
